coredata.dc:title: Preparation of waterborne anti-counterfeiting ink based on dual luminescent nanohybrids of bacterial cellulose nanocrystals and lanthanide‑nitrogen co-modified GQDs

coredata.dc:description: To address the growing challenge of counterfeit prevention, this study developed a novel anti-counterfeiting ink system based on bacterial cellulose nanocrystals (BCNC) and lanthanide (Er, Yb)‑nitrogen (N) co-dropped graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which exhibited both photoluminescence (PL) and upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) fluorescent properties as well as excellent rheological characteristics. The Er/Yb/N-GQDs with positive charges were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method and subsequently assembled with negatively charged BCNC through electrostatic self-assembly to fabricate a novel nanohybrid, Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC. Raman spectroscopy results indicated an enhancement in the graphitization of GQDs due to lanthanide modification. The TEM results demonstrated a homogeneous distribution of Er/Yb/N-GQDs on BCNC, while XRD, FTIR, and XPS analyses confirmed their physical binding, thus validating the successful synthesis of novel nanohybrids. Then, Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC was introduced into PVA waterborne ink and exhibited dual anti-counterfeiting properties by emitting blue fluorescence at Em 440 nm under Ex 370 nm and green fluorescence at Em 550 nm under Ex 980 nm. Furthermore, the incorporation of BCNC significantly enhanced the thixotropic behavior and yield stress of the PVA waterborne ink. This enhancement made the dual anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink more suitable for diversified applications on different devices and various substrates, thus providing a novel approach for convenient and rapid information encryption and high security anti-counterfeiting.

AFM images of BCNC (a) and the corresponding particle size distribution histogram (b); TEM images of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC (c); TEM (d), HR-TEM images (d, inset), corresponding particle size distribution histogram (e), AFM images and surface height (f) of Er/Yb/N-GQDs. EDX spectrum (g), and EDS images recorded for carbon (h), oxygen (i), Yb (j), and Er (k) of the Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNCs.

1 mg/mL N-GQDs and Er/Yb/N-GQDs as experimental samples. head.ce:title: Preparation of waterborne anti-counterfeiting ink based on dual luminescent nanohybrids of bacterial cellulose nanocrystals and lanthanide‑nitrogen co-modified GQDs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address the growing challenge of counterfeit prevention, this study developed a novel anti-counterfeiting ink system based on bacterial cellulose nanocrystals (BCNC) and lanthanide (Er, Yb)‑nitrogen (N) co-dropped graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which exhibited both photoluminescence (PL) and upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) fluorescent properties as well as excellent rheological characteristics. The Er/Yb/N-GQDs with positive charges were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method and subsequently assembled with negatively charged BCNC through electrostatic self-assembly to fabricate a novel nanohybrid, Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC. Raman spectroscopy results indicated an enhancement in the graphitization of GQDs due to lanthanide modification. The TEM results demonstrated a homogeneous distribution of Er/Yb/N-GQDs on BCNC, while XRD, FTIR, and XPS analyses confirmed their physical binding, thus validating the successful synthesis of novel nanohybrids. Then, Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC was introduced into PVA waterborne ink and exhibited dual anti-counterfeiting properties by emitting blue fluorescence at Em 440 nm under Ex 370 nm and green fluorescence at Em 550 nm under Ex 980 nm. Furthermore, the incorporation of BCNC significantly enhanced the thixotropic behavior and yield stress of the PVA waterborne ink. This enhancement made the dual anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink more suitable for diversified applications on different devices and various substrates, thus providing a novel approach for convenient and rapid information encryption and high security anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:para: With the proliferation of scalable technology and online open-source images, counterfeiters can manufacture completely identical replicas and place them alongside genuine products in any retail store [1]. Counterfeit products often feature falsified brand identifications, which can include even those elements designed for anti-counterfeiting, such as exclusive labels, quick response (QR) codes, and barcodes. They may also illegitimately use protected designations of origin or geographical indications [2]. Customers are deceived into purchasing counterfeit products, thereby posing a risk to health and brand reputation. Consequently, an increasing number of industries urgently require efficient anticounterfeiting technology. Photoluminescent (fluorescent and phosphorescent) compounds are the optimal materials for developing highly secure complex anti-counterfeit inks. These compounds encompass quantum dots, graphene or carbon dots, polymer nanoparticles, upconversion nanoparticles, organic and inorganic metal complexes [3]. Additionally, fluorescent cellulose materials have been extensively studied. Various types of cellulose materials can be combined with diverse carbon dots to generate novel materials with a wide range of characteristics, demonstrating potential prospects for highly efficient anti-counterfeiting and authentication technologies [4]. These materials in preventing counterfeiting have led researchers to develop luminescent materials with enhanced security and efficiency.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have become a promising and novel fluorescent material due to their outstanding photostability, tunable photoluminescence properties, and biocompatibility [5]. However, as-prepared GQDs have a low quantum yield (QY) and require an enhanced fluorescence intensity. To improve these properties, heteroatom doping has been demonstrated to be effective in altering the optical, electronic, and structural properties of GQDs [6]. Nitrogen, which has an atomic size comparable to that of carbon and a greater electronegativity (3.04) than carbon (2.55), is an ideal dopant for carbon materials [7]. Doping nitrogen into GQDs (N-GQDs) polarizes the material, which affects the fluorescence emission, leading to higher QY and excellent fluorescence quenching properties [8]. However, the doped GQDs still exhibit monochromatic fluorescence and cannot meet the multiple fluorescence characteristics required for advanced anti-counterfeiting measures.

Notably, lanthanide-doped materials have garnered significant attention due to their excellent properties, including a narrow emission band, a long lifetime, and a large Stokes shift [9]. Additionally, lanthanide-doped nanomaterials exhibit efficient spectral conversion capabilities and excellent luminescence properties through upconversion and downshift processes, leveraging the distinct 4fn orbitals and abundant energy-level structures of lanthanides [10]. Under near-infrared (NIR), visible light, or under ultraviolet (UV) illumination, they emit polychromatic radiation spanning the entire visible and NIR spectral region [11]. Yao et al. (2022) [12] employed a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method to elucidate the luminescence properties of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs, achieving a dual emission phenomenon encompassing both NIR and UV spectra. An anticounterfeiting ability was typically achieved by blending lanthanide elements such as Er3+ and Yb3+ with GQDs to enable dual UV and NIR excitation for control [13–15]. Hence, the incorporation of Er3+ doping within N-GQDs to induce more intricate luminescent behavior holds significant merit. Moreover, Yb3+ ions serve as a good choice of sensitizer for codoping alongside Er3+ ions owing to their absorption cross-section near 980 nm, which matches the energy difference of Er3+ [16–18]. This matching facilitates enhanced energy transfer between the activator and sensitizer, resulting in higher energy transfer probabilities, thereby amplifying upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL). Therefore, lanthanides can be considered for modifying GQDs to prepare advanced anticounterfeiting materials with multicolor regulation.

Bacterial cellulose nanocrystals (BCNC) have gained widespread attention due to their unique properties, including a large specific surface area, excellent optical clarity, good rheology modifiers, and the ability to act as reinforcing fillers for composites [19–22]. BCNC can exhibit thixotropic behavior, wherein they can become less viscous under shear stress and regain their viscosity when the stress is removed [23]. This property is desirable in various applications such as inks, paints, and biomedical materials. Moreover, BCNC acts as stabilizers, preventing particle aggregation and sedimentation, thereby maintaining a uniform dispersion within the liquid matrix [24]. This stability enhancement is valuable in anti-counterfeiting inks where long-term stability is required.

However, there have been few reports on the advanced anti-counterfeiting that use the BCNC to enhance the rheology of inks and combine modified GQDs with dual luminescence capabilities to improve the information security. In this study, Er/Yb and N co-modified GQDs with dual luminescence properties were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method and incorporated onto BCNCs (prepared by acid hydrolysis) through electrostatic self-assembly. The optimal ratio between Er/Yb/N-GQDs and BCNC in the nanohybrid was determined based on the fluorescence intensity resulting from the incorporation of various doses of Er/Yb/N-GQDs onto BCNC. The structural and morphological features along with the optical behavior of N-GQDs, Er/Yb/N-GQDs, BCNC, and Er/Yb/N-GQD-BCNC were characterized. Then, the Er/Yb/N-GQD-BCNC were incorporated into poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) waterborne inks, followed by an assessment of their rheological behavior. However, the molecular mechanisms underlying the change in rheological behavior necessitate further investigation. The as-prepared BCNC could serve as carriers for Er/Yb/N-GQDs in PVA waterborne inks, facilitating the successful development of PVA waterborne fluorescent inks with improved rheological properties and dual anti-counterfeiting properties through UV and NIR excitation mechanisms. This study will contribute to advancing information security and identity authentication technologies, particularly in printing and packaging industries. The developed ink holds promising for systematic application across different devices and substrates, offering enhanced security features against counterfeit activities.

All chemicals were used without any further purification. Sodium stearyl sulfate (SSS, purity ≥98.0 %, AR), Antifoam 204 (Containing 100 % active ingredients, a mixture of organic non‑silicone polypropylene-based polyether dispersions), and PVA (Type 224, alcoholysis degree: 87.0–89.0 mol%, viscosity: 40.0–48.0 mPa.s) were obtained from Rhawn (Shanghai, China). Erbium chloride hexahydrate (ErCl3·6H2O, purity ≥99.9 %, AR), ytterbium chloride hexahydrate (YbCl3·6H2O, purity ≥99.9 %, AR), citric acid monohydrate (CA, purity ≥99.5 %, AR), and urea (purity ≥99.0 %, AR) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Cellulose ester (CE) dialysis bags (MWCO: 100–500 Da) were purchased from Biorigin (Beijing, China). BC nanofiber membranes in the form of gel-like pellicles were produced by Qihong Tech. Co. (Guilin, Guangxi, China). All solutions were prepared with deionized water.

Er/Yb/N-GQDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. Specifically, CA (1.4 g), urea (1.2 g), ErCl3·6H2O (0.03 g), and YbCl3·6H2O (0.3 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and stirred for 10 min with a magnetic stirrer to form a precursor solution. The precursor solution was then transferred to a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 160 °C for 5 h in a high-temperature vacuum oven. The yellow–brown liquid obtained from the reaction was subsequently placed into a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500–1000 Da) and dialyzed for 24 h with water changes every 12 h. The resulting light-green liquid was then freeze-dried to obtain Er/Yb/N-GQDs. The preparation process of N-GQDs was consistent with the aforementioned procedure. The optimal molar ratio of CA to urea was determined based on the fluorescence intensity of the prepared N-GQDS (Fig. S1). In addition, determination of optimal conditions for the synthesis process was detailed in the supplementary document.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BCNC and fabrication of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: A BCNC suspension was prepared according to the method described by Ngoensawat et al. (2021) [3] with certain modifications. Dried BC membrane (1.0 g) was trimmed and processed into fragments. Subsequently, the fragments were loaded into a centrifuge tube, and 10 mL of 60 % (w/w) sulfuric acid was added and homogenized for 30 min. Centrifuged the BCNC suspension at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, washed the precipitate, and repeated this procedure several times until the suspension reached neutrality. Finally, the suspension was freeze-dried to obtain the BCNC.

Assembly of nanohybrids: Er/Yb/N-GQDs (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, 1.50, 2.00, 2.50, 3.00, and 4.00 mg) was added to the BCNC suspension (1 mL, 1 mg/mL). The solution was then diluted with deionized water to a specific volume and stirred at room temperature for 1 h to obtain the nanohybrid. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged three times to remove any excess unreacted Er/Yb/N-GQDs until the washed supernatant showed no fluorescence under UV light. The nanohybrids were redispersed, and the optimal addition amount of Er/Yb/N-GQDs was determined according to the fluorescence intensity. The resulting precipitates was freeze-dried to obtain nanocrystals of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the PVA waterborne fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: The PVA waterborne ink was prepared following the method established by Li et al. (2020) [24] with specific adaptations. SSS (1.0 wt%) was chosen as the surfactant. Subsequently, 1.0 wt% of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC nanohybrid was gradually added into the PVA waterborne ink, which prepared according to the aforementioned method. The resultant blend underwent gentle stirring at 45 °C for 1 h to obtain the Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC-PVA waterborne fluorescent ink.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was performed from 5° to 80° with Cu/Kα radiation (λ = 1.5404 Å). The chemical structure of the nanomaterials was analyzed with a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Bruker-VERTEX 70, Germany) over a wavelength range of 400–4000 cm − 1. Raman scattering measurements were performed using a Nanofinder 30 spectrometer (Tokyo Instruments Co., Osaka, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used for elemental analysis of the Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC utilizing a PHI 5300 electron spectrometer operating at 14 kV and 250 W with Mg K-alpha radiation (PE Ltd., USA). The morphological and structural characteristics of nanomaterials were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HITACHIH-7650, Japan) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Multimode 8-Bruker, Germany). An Edinburgh Model FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, England) was employed for the measurement of photoluminescence (PL) spectra (sample holder: SC-05) and QY (sample holder: SC-30). UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy was conducted using a Lambda 25 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Massachusetts, USA) within the wavelength range of 200 to 700 nm, employing a 1 cm quartz cuvette. A NANOTRAC WAVE II/ZETA (Microtrac, USA) was used to measure the zeta potential of the sample.

An AR 1500 rotational rheometer (TA Instruments, USA) was used to conduct the rheological measurements. The experiment utilized an aluminum Peltier plate (PP25–40 mm) with a parallel plate geometry, where the interspace was configured to be 1 mm, and was preceded by a pre-shearing manipulation (shear rate: 1 s−1 to 60 s) to ensure uniformity in the initial suspended state. According to the meticulous three-step procedure devised by Hoeng et al. (2017) [25] the study began by subjecting the ink to a range of low shear rates (2 s-1 to 60 s-1), simulating its quiescent state on the screen. Following this, a high shear regime (1000 s−1 to 15 s−1) was applied to the ink to quantify the rate of recovery from structural damage within the suspension. Last, a new period of low shear rates (2 s−1 to 150 s−1) was introduced to represent the ink reconstitution post-printing.

To delve deeper into the rheological properties of the ink, an investigation encompassing strain scan experiments, dynamic viscoelasticity measurements, and viscosity-temperature characteristic assessments was conducted. All the described rheological performance assessments were conducted at ambient room temperature (20 °C ~ 25 °C).

Data were processed and subjected to analysis of variance (ANOVA). Data visualization was performed using Microsoft Office, Origin 2022, and Python (3.8.3) in PyCharm. The schematic diagram in this study was made with Blender (3.4) and BioRender software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characteristics of nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1a depicts a schematic of the process for synthesizing the dual-emissive Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC nanohybrid material via electrostatic binding. Positively charged Er/Yb/N-GQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method, incorporating N doping and Er coordination. An amide bond is formed through a dehydration condensation reaction between the amino group (−NH2) of urea and the carboxyl group (–COOH) of CA [26]. The coordination bond arises from the attractive forces between Er3+ ions (positive charge) and carboxyl functional groups (negative charge). The transformation of the BC membrane into BCNC occurs via the hydrolytic action of H2SO4. Due to the acid hydrolytic activity of H2SO4, it can concurrently cleave cellulose chains and esterify the hydroxy groups (–OH) of cellulose with sulfate groups ( −
OSO 3 −
), thereby generating needle-like or rod-like cellulose nanostructures with a pronounced negative surface charge [27]. Finally, by blending Er/Yb/N-GQDs and BCNC in water, Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC nanohybrid nanohybrids can be synthesized. The relatively large structure and negatively charged surface of BCNC effectively facilitate incorporating of Er/Yb/N-GQDs through electrostatic attraction between −
OSO 3 −
and −
NH 3 +
. The suspension of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC was imperceptible to the naked eye under daylight conditions but exhibited intense PL with typical behavior under UV irradiation (Fig. 5a). Thus, Er/Yb/N-GQDs were successfully incorporated onto the surface of BCNC (Fig. 2c). Subsequently, we introduced a waterborne fluorescent ink containing Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC nanohybrids into the ink cartridge of a stamper, followed by imprinting on filter paper (Fig. 1b). The stamped filter paper exhibited no discernible patterns when exposed to sunlight, whereas it manifested a pattern of blue fluorescence under 370 nm UV light and distinctly displayed green fluorescence when illuminated by a 980 nm infrared laser pointer. This observation highlighted the prospective utilization of the waterborne fluorescent ink containing Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC as an advanced dual anticounterfeiting measure.

Fig. 2a presents an AFM image of BCNC, illustrating characteristic nanostructures with dimensions ranging from 350 to 1000 nm in length and 30 to 60 nm in width (Fig. 2b & S2). The TEM image (Fig. 2c) of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC illustrated the dispersion of black granular GQDs across the surface of BCNC. The TEM image coupled with the corresponding size distribution (Fig. 2d) showed that GQDs co-modified with Er/Yb and N exhibited a nearly spherical morphology, with an average dimension of 4.32 nm. Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2d inset) elucidated the crystalline nature of modified GQDs, showing a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (102) crystal plane of the sp2 graphite carbon structure [28,29]. The AFM image (Fig. 2f) provided additional insight into the quasispherical morphology of modified GQDs, with a characteristic topographical height ranging from 1.0 to 3.0 nm. The above results indicated successful incorporating of Er/Yb/N-GQDs onto the surface of BCNC. Additionally, elemental maps obtained from energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) spectroscopy (Fig. 2 g-k) showed a uniform distribution of C, O, Er, and Yb across the nanohybrid structure of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC.

Subsequent Raman microscopy was employed for further structural characterization, and Raman spectra of the prepared N-GQDs and Er/Yb/N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 3a. In this context, the D band (1305 cm−1, 1248 cm−1) was attributed to sp3 disordered hybridized carbon due to structural defects and dangling and edge sp2 carbon bonds that disrupted the symmetry, while the G band (1596 cm−1, 1580 cm−1) corresponded to hybridized carbon resulting from the in-plane stretching vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms [30]. The intensity of the D band was correlated with the quantity of the sp3 phase, while the intensity of the G band was correlated with the quantity of the sp2 phase, indicating that oxygen and N functional groups contributed to an increase in the disordered structure of N-GQDs prepared in the samples [31]. In the Raman spectrum of Er/Yb/N-GQDs, the intensity of the D band decreased, and a low-frequency shift was observed in the specific peak position of the sp3 phase, while an enhancement in the intensity of the G band was observed. The introduction of Er atoms induced alterations in the local carbon atom hybridization structure, reducing structural defects in sp3 hybridized carbon atoms through coordination with carboxyl functional groups, thereby generating a higher abundance of ordered sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. Notably, ID/IG signified the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon atoms, serving as an indicator of the graphitization extent in carbon materials. Consequently, ID/IG(Er/Yb/N-GQDs) < 1 < ID/IG(N-GQDs) indicated a higher degree of graphitization and an enhanced QY in Er/Yb/N-GQDs (confirmed by the fluorescence intensity in Fig. 4a).

The samples were further subjected to analysis using FTIR spectroscopy, with the spectra shown in Fig. S3a. The spectrum of BCNC exhibited a broad peak within the range of 3750–3000 cm−1, indicative of the OH stretching vibration, a hydroxyl group feature commonly present in cellulose and lignin [32]. The spectral peak observed at 1308 cm−1 was attributed to the CC stretching vibration. Additionally, the bands at 1164 cm−1 and 1060 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the C-O-C glycosidic linkage and the C-OH extension vibration of secondary alcohols in cellulose, respectively [33,34]. These characteristics were also evident in the spectrum of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC. The N-GQD spectrum exhibited a stretching band at 1402 cm−1 corresponding to CC bonds and another stretching band at 1594 cm−1 associated with CO bonds, indicating a certain degree of graphitization in the material. Additionally, a stretching band related to NH vibrations was observed at 3169 cm−1, confirming successful N atom doping [35]. The introduction of lanthanide elements enhanced the functional group absorption intensity in Er/Yb/N-GQDs compared to N-GQDs in their spectra. The noticeable increase in the CC stretching band was related to the degree of graphitization (confirmed by Raman spectroscopy). Vibrational bands observed at approximately 1247 cm−1, 1625 cm−1, and 3191 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CN, CO, and NH bonds, respectively, indicating that the nanocomposite of Er/Yb/N-GQDs was rich in amide bonds and exhibited substantial surface loading of –NH2 groups [36]. The spectrum of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC revealed distinct stretching vibrations of NH and CN moieties at 3264 cm−1 and 1162 cm−1, respectively, indicative of the successful incorporating of Er/Yb/N-GQDs onto BCNC.

XRD patterns of the synthesized samples were recorded by scanning the 2θ angle from 0° to 60°, as illustrated in Fig. 3c. The diffraction pattern of N-GQDs exhibited a broadened reflection peak at 22.8°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite carbon (Fig. S3b). It is noteworthy that in comparison to N-GQDs, the peak of Er/Yb/N-GQDs exhibited a rightward shift (26.9°). This phenomenon occurred due to the doping of Er/Yb, which resulted in the formation of a greater number of nitrogen-containing functional groups (confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy). The greater electronegativity and smaller atomic radius of nitrogen compared to carbon induced lattice contraction, consequently reducing the interplanar spacing [37,38]. The XRD pattern of BCNC showed peaks at 2θ values of 14.4°, 16.8°, 22.6°, and 35.1°, corresponding to the crystallographic planes 101, 10‍
i ¯
, 002, and 040, respectively, in alignment with the characteristic cellulose I crystalline phase [39]. The remaining peaks were attributed to the amorphous contribution. In comparison to BCNC, the intensity of the (002) plane diffraction in Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC was notably enhanced. This enhancement could be attributed to alignment between the characteristic diffraction peak region of GQDs and that of nanocellulose (002), indicating uniform embedding of GQDs within the BCNC matrix.

To substantiate the chemical composition of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC, XPS analysis was conducted on the sample, revealing the presence of Er, Yb, C, N, and O, as shown in Fig. 3c. The peaks of Er 4d and Yb 4d were located at 200.03 eV and 175.48 eV, respectively, with corresponding contents of 0.38 % and 3.57 %. The Er/Yb/N-doped GQDs-BCNC exhibited distinct peaks corresponding to C, N, and O at energy levels of 284 eV, 401 eV, and 533 eV, with respective contents of 50.22 %, 11.48 %, and 34.35 %. The high-resolution multiscan spectra for the C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s regions of the Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC nanohybrids were subjected to curve fitting (Fig. 3d-f). The binding energies of the peaks of 286.4 eV, 287.3 eV, and 284.5 eV, as indicated in Fig. 3d, corresponded to the carbon structures of C=C/C-C, C-O/C-N, and CO, respectively. The N 1 s spectrum could be resolved into a pair of distinct peaks, with one attributed to pyrrolic N and the other to graphitic or quaternary N atoms, located at binding energies of 400.4 and 402.0 eV. In the O1s spectrum depicted in Fig. 3f, two well-fitted peaks located at 532.3.7 and 533.5 eV were attributed to the CO and C–OH/C–O–C functional groups, respectively. This result indicated the predominant presence of carbon and oxygen as primary constituents in the GQDs, as well as the successful doping of nitrogen atoms. Moreover, the surface of Er/Yb/N-GQDs contained carboxyl groups and free amines. Interestingly, the presence of CO in the C1s region as well as the disappearance of the amine peak in the N1s region were consistent with the FTIR results and indicated successful incorporating of Er/Yb/N-GQDs onto the BCNC surface.

Fig. 4a shows the UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs (1 mg/mL) and Er/Yb/N-GQDs (1 mg/mL). Fig. 4a (lower subplot) presents the UV absorption spectra for the preparation of N-GQDs and Er/Yb/N-GQDs, with the main absorption peak occurring at 242 nm attributed to the π → π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 domains (C=C) within the N-GQDs structure [39]. Additionally, the absorption peaks within the 300–400 nm range were ascribed to the n → π* transitions of oxidized groups such as CO, epoxy resin, and CO, located at the edges of the N-GQDs [40]. The conspicuous enhancement of absorption peaks within the 300–400 nm range for Er/Yb/N-GQDs signified augmented generation of oxygen-containing functional groups along the edges of N-GQDs under the influence of Er/Yb. According to the reports of Kumar et al. (2022) [41], the inherent aromatic π system not serving as an efficient emission center results in a lack of significant fluorescence emission at approximately 250 nm for GQDs excitation. However, in this study, N-GQDs exhibited weak blue fluorescence at 250 nm, indicating that the presence of nitrogen functional groups influenced the energy level structure of GQDs.

To elucidate the optical characteristics of N-GQDs, an exhaustive PL spectral analysis was conducted. The results demonstrated that the luminescence spectrum of N-GQDs was correlated with the excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission peak gradually shifted toward longer wavelengths, indicating excitation-dependent PL behavior of N-GQDs. In response to a change in the excitation wavelength from 370 nm to 420 nm, the Er/Yb/N-GQDs and N-GQDs displayed shifts in their peak emission wavelengths, transitioning from 440 nm to 450 nm and 480 nm, respectively (Fig. 4b & c). The modulation of excitation wavelengths may potentially impact subsequent charge separation, alter the distribution of excited state energy, and impose limitations on hole and electron movement at the surface of N-GQDs, influencing their radiative recombination [42]. The emission region with a narrow photoluminescence range (440–450 nm) confirmed the relatively narrow size distribution of CQDs, attributed to similar quantum effects and emission traps present on the surface [43].

Fig. 4d depicts the chromaticity coordinates of Er/Yb/N-GQDs within the chromaticity diagram, showcasing adjustable luminescent hues as recorded by the CIE. These colors range from point A (X = 0.14, Y = 0.11) to point B (X = 0.32, Y = 0.52). The fluorescence spectra of Er/Yb/N co-codoped GQDs in normalized form exhibited the persistent manifestation of wavelength-dependent fluorescence characteristics, indicative of the intrinsic properties of N-GQDs. Moreover, under identical concentration conditions (1 mg/mL), Er/Yb/N-GQDs exhibited a significantly enhanced fluorescence intensity at an emission wavelength of 440 nm upon excitation at 370 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 4a (upper subplot, green line). After analysis, the introduction of Er/Yb was found to lead to a substantial enhancement in the fluorescence QY of N-GQDs from 15 % to 31 %, thereby demonstrating heightened fluorescence attributes. Significantly, upon excitation by 980 nm infrared radiation, the emission spectrum of Er/Yb/N-GQDs peaked at 556 nm, indicating this material exhibited both PL and UCPL characteristics. The luminous phenomenon is shown in Fig. 4f, with the sequence from left to right corresponding to N-GQDs (daylight, Ex 370 nm) and Er/Yb/N-GQDs (Ex 370 nm, Ex 980 nm). Moreover, the emission spectrum of N-GQDs in Fig. S4 under 980 nm excitation indicated that the manifestation of UCPL was not intrinsic to the N-GQDs.

The luminescence mechanisms of N-GQDs and Er/Yb/N-GQDs can be summarized as depicted in Fig. 4e. After irradiation with light of a wavelength of 370 nm, N-GQDs experienced an energy level shift of 25,000 cm-1, transitioning from their ground state to a singlet excited state (1π-π*). The 440 nm emission band is generally attributed to the 3π-π* triplet-state energy level of N-GQDs [44,45]. According to the results of Zhang et al. (2017) [46], energy transfer occurs through the 1LC pathway depicted in Fig. 4e when the energy gap ΔE (1π-π* ~ 3π-π*) is <5000 cm−1. Subsequently, Yb3+ ions continuously absorbed photons from an infrared laser (Ex 980 nm) and were elevated to an excited energy level (2F5/2), and energy was then transferred to Er3+ ions before Yb3+ ions returned to their ground state (2F7/2). The outcome involved energy transfer driving the transition of Er3+ ions from the ground state 4I15/2 level to the 4I11/2 state, followed by transitions through the 4I13/2 level to the 4F9/2(1) state. Alternatively, a direct transition from the 4I11/2 to 4F7/2 level caused electrons to undergo multiphoton relaxation, resulting in nonradiative decay to the lower energy state 4S3/2 [12–15,47]. Ultimately, electrons underwent radiative transitions from states 4S3/2 and 4F9/2(1) to the ground state, inducing emissions at 556 nm and 673 nm (the green light is much stronger than the red light), respectively.

To optimize the luminescence intensity of the nanohybrid materials, the optimal loading of Er/Yb/N-GQDs onto BCNC needed to be ascertained, thereby establishing the optimal stoichiometric ratio. Hence, we added different weight powders of Er/Yb/N-GQDs into BCNC suspensions with constant volume and concentration, yielding nanohybrids that distinctly exhibited luminescence upon exposure to UV light (Fig. 5a). The fluorescence spectra and peak intensity profiles of nanohybrid suspensions incorporating various quantities of Er/Yb/N-GQDs were observed upon excitation at 370 nm (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence intensity increased with increasing amount of Er/Yb/N-GQDs, reaching its apex at an addition of 2 mg and subsequently declining to a relatively steady level with further mass increments. The observed trend aligns remarkably well with the results of zeta potential measurements (Fig. 5c), exhibiting a strong congruence. As depicted in Fig. 1a, the H2SO4-hydrolyzed BCNC exhibited negatively charged −
OSO 3 −
moieties, whereas the Er/Yb/N-GQDs were characterized by positively charged −
NH 3 +
groups. The zeta potential of pure BCNC suspensions was recorded as −33.4 mV. Due to the charge neutralization effect, the incorporation of Er/Yb/N-GQDs and their deposition onto BCNC led to a reduction in the negative surface charge of the nanohybrid material. Upon the addition of a minimum of 2 mg of Er/Yb/N-GQDs, saturation of the BCNC surface of Er/Yb/N-GQD-BCNC nanohybrids was achieved. The optimum zeta potential value of the Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC nanohybrid was +52.4 mV, indicating its high stability and excellent dispersion characteristics, rendering it well suited for use as an ink stored in a suspended liquid form.

According to the laser confocal images presented in Fig. S5, the BCNC exhibited no fluorescence under excitation at 370 nm (Fig. S5a), whereas the Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC nanohybrid displayed pronounced blue fluorescence (Fig. S5b). Under the external laser excitation, luminescence in the blue light region was facilitated by defects on the surface of Er/Yb/N-GQDs and their abundant polar functional groups, such as carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups [48,49]. The Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC nanohybrid material demonstrated a distinct dendritic morphology, abundant fluorescence points, and a uniform color distribution on the surface, providing further evidence of the successful introduction of Er/Yb/N-GQDs into the BCNC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the rheological behavior of the Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC ink
ce:section.ce:para: The Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC exhibited excellent fluorescence intensity and photostability, rendering them suitable for use as invisible security ink. In this study, the Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC exhibiting superior performance necessitates excellent dispersion within the solvent to ensure optimal formability during sequential printing. Notably, the PVA waterborne ink demonstrated a shear-thinning behavior as a non-Newtonian fluid, exhibiting superior adaptability for printing due to its high viscosity at low shear rates and low viscosity under high-speed agitation [50]. Among the tested formulations, the rheological behavior of the waterborne fluorescent ink composed of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC-PVA was investigated. The thixotropic property of the PVA waterborne ink remained nearly constant, as evidenced by the viscosity exhibiting minimal sensitivity to variations in the shear rate (Fig. 6a). However, the viscosity of the Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC-PVA hybrid solution exhibited an initial increase followed by a gradual decrease, demonstrating a notable shear-thinning characteristic (Fig. 6b). In the printing transfer process, friction occurred between the ink and the ink roller [51]. The introduction of BCNC imparted excellent thixotropy, which was characterized by enhanced fluidity and extensibility, to the PVA waterborne ink. During high-speed agitation, the viscosity of the ink decreased, resulting in reduced friction between the ink and the ink roller, thus improving the overall flow characteristics and lowering the energy consumption. After the ink was transferred onto the paper, due to external force loss, the ink viscosity increased, and its excellent extensibility facilitated improved adherence to the paper. Based on the aforementioned characteristics, the ink, when applied to the substrate, exhibited minimal spillage into the surrounding areas, thereby yielding a well-defined imprint on the printing medium. Significantly, during the transition from high to low shear in Fig. 6b, the viscosity of the composite ink swiftly reverted to its original level within a brief timeframe (approximately 15 s), indicating alterations in the internal rheological properties of the PVA waterborne ink during high-speed shear. Furthermore, the incorporation of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC accelerated the recovery of the structural properties of the ink after the change, endowing the fluorescent ink with excellent thixotropic behavior.

Fig. 6c depicts the strain scan test conducted on the Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC-PVA waterborne ink. The prominently wide area positioned to the left of the delineated red line signifies the linear viscoelastic phase within the ink system. This area indicated robust intermolecular interactions among the polymer chains present in the composite ink, resulting in the formation of a homogeneous solution system. The amplitude scan results at low frequencies (Fig. 6d) indicated that the solution consistently exhibited a shear-thinning capability within the linear viscoelastic region throughout the entire testing procedure. As the angular frequency increased from 0.1 rad/s to 300 rad/s, the storage modulus and loss modulus of the system correspondingly increased, resulting in a linear decrease in the complex viscosity and an enhanced shear-thinning ability. Based on the above, the frequency dependence of the storage modulus of the composite ink was relatively low, while the loss modulus significantly increased with increasing frequency. The complex viscosity of the composite ink system exhibited a gradual transition from a solid-like to a gel-like state, highlighting its viscoelastic characteristics conducive to large-scale continuous inkjet printing.

Figs. 6e & f depict small-amplitude temperature rise tests conducted on both PVA and Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC-PVA waterborne inks, presenting an assessment of their curing performance under oscillatory conditions. The gelation temperature of the PVA waterborne ink was 40.3 °C, whereas the corresponding gelation temperature of the composite ink augmented with Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC nanohybrids increased by 3.3 °C. This enhancement could be attributed to the abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC nanohybrids imparting them with excellent water-absorption and water-retention properties [52]. Consequently, a greater amount of heat was required to eliminate this moisture during the drying process. Additionally, the viscosity of fluids closely correlates with temperature coefficients [53]. As the temperature increased, both solutions exhibited an initial rise in damping coefficients, followed by a subsequent decline. This pattern indicates that the viscosity of these solutions reached its peak values at 34.1 °C and 40.3 °C, respectively, gradually decreasing thereafter. In summary, the incorporation of Er/Yb/N-GQD-BCNC nanohybrids elevated the temperature required for curing the PVA waterborne ink system and prolonged the time needed for complete ink drying. Consequently, this led to an increase in the energy consumption necessary for the process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC composites ink for advanced anticounterfeiting applications
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the rheological properties exhibited by the aforementioned Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC-PVA composite ink, we subsequently demonstrated its operational performance in actual applications. Fig. 1b illustrates the application of composite ink within a stamper, showcasing a multitude of elements such as English letters, Chinese characters, numerals, and artistic patterns. The synthesized Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC exhibited luminescent properties in PVA waterborne ink, emitting stable and bright coloration under both UV and 980 nm NIR laser irradiation. Subsequently, we injected composite ink into a whiteboard marker for writing/printing on different packaging materials (Fig. 7 ). Notably, the presentation of the NIR fluorescence images in Fig. 7 was not readily due to the narrow optical pathway of the 980 nm NIR laser and the absence of equipment suitable for large-area irradiation. Fig. 7 shows the printing performance of the fluorescent ink on various substrates, including kraft paper, a food storage container (polypropylene, PP), an aluminum-coated plastic film, a plastic bottle (polyethylene terephthalate, PET), a soda can (aluminum), a cling film (polyethylene, PE or low-density polyethylene, LDPE), and a glass bottle, each exhibiting remarkably pronounced effects. Hence, this fluorescent ink possessed exceptional fluorescence-based anticounterfeiting characteristics, rendering it highly applicable across a myriad of domains encompassing information transmission and counterfeit deterrence.

In summary, we developed a novel anti-counterfeiting ink based on Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC. the synthesized Er/Yb/N-GQDs demonstrated both PL (blue light) and UCPL (green light) characteristics upon excitation at wavelengths of 370 nm and 980 nm, respectively. Therefore, effective information extraction necessitated the concurrent use of two distinct excitation light sources, significantly increased the difficulty of illicit counterfeiting. Besides, the electrostatic self-assembly was used to fabricate the Er/Yb/N-GQDs-BCNC novel nanohybrids with dual luminescent, followed by their incorporation into a PVA waterborne ink that. Leveraging the unique rheological characteristics of BCNC, the nanohybrids contribute to improved thixotropic behavior and increased yield stress of the ink, facilitating its systematic application on various devices and substrates. The resulting stable network architecture enhanced the printability and resistance to shear-thinning of ink, but the molecular mechanism of the change in rheological behavior needed further exploration. This study not only introduced a novel anticounterfeiting solution but also provided valuable insights for its integration into printing and packaging industries, contributing significantly to advancements in information security and authentication technologies.
